Lung squamous cell carcinoma is a common type of lung cancer characterized by abnormal cell proliferation in the lung epithelium. High-resolution imaging can reveal several key features of this disease. Nuclear pleomorphism refers to the variability in nuclear size and shape, which can indicate different stages of cellular transformation. Intercellular bridges, visible desmosomal connections between squamous cells, suggest areas of increased cell proliferation and potential tumor invasion. Immune cell aggregates, or tertiary lymphoid structures, near tumor regions may reflect the presence of immune cells infiltrating the tumor, which can influence the disease's progression and treatment response. Additionally, microcystic or small gland-like structures scattered in certain regions may indicate the presence of residual or regrowing lung epithelial cells, which can complicate diagnosis and management.

Lung squamous cell carcinoma, as a common type of lung cancer, is characterized by its aggressive growth and poor prognosis. Under high-resolution imaging, the description of lung squamous cell carcinoma can be summarized from four key perspectives: Dense cytoplasm, Polygonal tumor cells, Necrotic regions, and Regions of necrosis surrounded by viable tumor cells. Dense cytoplasm refers to the thick, gelatinous cytoplasm that is often seen in the tumor cells, which is a hallmark of the aggressive progression of the disease. Polygonal tumor cells describe the well-defined, often angular shapes of the tumor cells, which may indicate a more aggressive growth pattern. Necrotic regions refer to the areas of cell death within the tumor masses, which may be associated with the presence of dead cells or areas of necrosis. Regions of necrosis surrounded by viable tumor cells describe the presence of areas where the tumor has died, but the surrounding areas are still viable, indicating the presence of a heterogeneous tumor with both dead and living cells.
